Name: reviewforge_openreview
Title: ReviewForge: Aligning and Stress-Testing LLM Peer Reviewers Against Large-Scale Human Reviews Without Manual Annotation
Short Hypothesis: Prior AI-review benchmarks either rely on small hand-annotated rubrics or ignore real peer-review behavior. By mining OpenReview at scale, we obtain noisy but authentic human ratings and review text for the same papers, enabling automatic alignment metrics (score regression, semantic agreement) alongside a unified robustness suite (format noise, multimodal mismatch, prompt injection). A single framework that couples **human-aligned evaluation** with **adversarial stress tests** is both novel and actionable for the community.
Related Work: MMReview and related benchmarks evaluate capability slices but not end-to-end alignment to venue-specific human judgments at scale. HELM/BIG-bench are not review-specific. Adversarial work on reviewers (e.g., in-paper prompt injection) evaluates attacks in isolation. ReviewForge synthesizes: (i) large-scale weak supervision from OpenReview, (ii) multi-axis robustness, and (iii) multi-model leaderboards—addressing the gap between toy tasks and deployable evaluation.
Abstract: We introduce ReviewForge, an evaluation framework for AI-assisted scientific peer review that requires no bespoke human studies. We crawl public OpenReview records to pair papers with human overall ratings and review text, then measure how well LLM/VLM reviewers predict human scores and overlap with human comments (embedding-based and lexical metrics). In parallel, we apply controlled perturbations—OCR-style noise, LaTeX stripping, caption–figure swaps, and hidden prompt-injection strings—and measure degradation in faithfulness and score stability. We report consistency across seeds and instruction paraphrases. ReviewForge yields a reproducible leaderboard and exposes when models appear strong on clean text yet collapse under realistic noise or attacks. The contribution is methodological and empirical: a scalable path to **alignment-aware** and **security-aware** evaluation of review models.
Experiments: 1) **OpenReview corpus**: Filter venues with public reviews (e.g., ICLR/NeurIPS/ICML where available); deduplicate by paper ID; retain papers with PDF or arXiv source. 2) **Models**: 3–5 frontier APIs plus 1–2 open models (e.g., Llama-class) with fixed prompts via Ai-Review-style templates. 3) **Alignment metrics**: MAE/Spearman between model-assigned scores and human ratings; semantic similarity (e.g., embedding cosine) between generated and human reviews; optional segment-level overlap. 4) **Robustness**: For each perturbation type, compute relative drop vs. clean on the same metrics; report injection success rate for score inflation. 5) **Consistency**: Variance across 5 seeds and 3 instruction paraphrases. 6) **Ablations**: Text-only vs. VLM with PDF images for format-noise settings.
Risk Factors and Limitations: OpenReview coverage is incomplete and ratings are noisy; we mitigate with large N and confidence intervals. Venue heterogeneity limits cross-venue claims—we stratify by venue/year. API cost is bounded by subsampling and caching. Crawling must respect OpenReview terms of use and robots policy.

Name: skepticbench_critical
Title: SkepticBench: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Critical Review Reasoning Beyond Surface Faithfulness
Short Hypothesis: Faithfulness and ROUGE-style overlap do not measure whether an AI reviewer can act as a **skeptical scientist**: spotting internal contradictions, unsupported claims, and methodological red flags. We unify programmatically injected error families—text–text conflict, text–figure inconsistency, overclaim, and procedural flaws—into one benchmark with binary and span-level automatic scoring. This raises the bar from incremental metric tweaks to a coherent **critical-reasoning** capability target suitable for top-tier ML venues.
Related Work: ContraReview-style work focuses only on contradictions; separate 'trick paper' ideas address method flaws in isolation. MMReview spreads many shallow tasks. SkepticBench contributes a **principled taxonomy** of review-critical failures, shared infrastructure for injection on arXiv CS papers, and joint reporting—positioning the work as a foundational reasoning benchmark rather than a single-axis test.
Abstract: We present SkepticBench, evaluating whether models can detect injected scientific issues during full-paper review. Starting from real arXiv papers with figures, we generate variants with controlled defects: (A) cross-section numeric or claim inconsistency, (B) caption–visual mismatch for VLMs, (C) overclaims relative to results, (D) methodological issues (e.g., data leakage description, invalid baseline). Models are prompted for structured review with explicit inconsistency flags. We report detection accuracy, precision/recall on localized spans (where applicable), and false-positive rates on clean papers. We compare LLMs on text to VLMs on PDFs to quantify multimodal reasoning gaps. SkepticBench maps directly to peer-review competence and supports future training or RL from programmatic rewards without human labels.
Experiments: 1) **Corpus**: 150–300 CS papers with extractable sections and figures (arXiv LaTeX when possible). 2) **Injection pipeline**: Rule-based and template-based edits with deterministic ground-truth labels. 3) **Evaluation**: Task-specific detection F1; FP on originals; breakdown by defect class; VLM vs. LLM comparison on figure-related tasks. 4) **Analysis**: Failure modes (e.g., subtle numeric drift vs. blatant contradiction); correlation with model size and chain-of-thought use.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Synthetic defects may not match naturally occurring errors—we position as a lower bound on capability and release injection code for extension. Parsing LaTeX fails on some papers; fallback to PDF text for LLM-only track.

Name: chainbreak_reasoning_attack
Title: ChainBreak: Analyzing How Prompt Injection Corrupts Multi-Step Reasoning in AI-Generated Peer Review
Short Hypothesis: Attack papers on AI reviewers often measure only final accept/reject scores. We hypothesize that injections **distort intermediate analysis** (strengths/weaknesses/evidence) while preserving fluent text, making harm invisible to outcome-only metrics. We formalize **reasoning corruption**: mis-attributed quotes, flipped weakness labels, or fabricated limitations—checked automatically against the paper via entailment and span grounding. This is a security + interpretability contribution at the level expected at AI conferences.
Related Work: Prompt-injection demos show score bias but rarely structure intermediate outputs for auditing. Chain-of-thought monitoring in general LLM safety differs in task structure. ChainBreak is review-specific: structured sections, citation to manuscript, and automatic verification against source text—yielding a new evaluation protocol and insights for hardened review assistants.
Abstract: We study prompt injection in scientific peer review beyond score manipulation. Given injected adversarial strings in the manuscript, we elicit structured reviews (strengths, weaknesses, questions) and decompose them into atomic claims. We automatically verify each claim against retrieved sentences from the paper using NLI/entailment models and span overlap. We define **reasoning corruption rate**: fraction of intermediate claims that are unsupported or contradicted despite a confident tone. We compare injection placements, paraphrase defenses, and multi-model families. ChainBreak shows that models can output plausible-looking reviews with corrupted reasoning chains—a failure mode critical for deployment—and suggests mitigations (e.g., forced citation spans, retrieval-grounded review).
Experiments: 1) **Base papers**: Subset of arXiv CS from SkepticBench or ReviewForge pipeline. 2) **Attacks**: Static and paraphrased injection strings seeking positive bias; control without injection. 3) **Structured output**: JSON or tagged sections for parseability. 4) **Verification**: Automatic NLI between each bullet and top-k retrieved sentences; manual spot-check is optional—not required for main results. 5) **Metrics**: Final score shift vs. reasoning corruption rate; correlation between the two. 6) **Mitigations**: Retrieval-augmented prompting vs. baseline.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Automatic NLI is imperfect; we report agreement on a small developer-set if needed (can be done by authors without a formal user study). Attacks do not cover all real adversaries; framework is extensible.

Name: panelbench_deliberation
Title: PanelBench: Do Multi-Agent Deliberation and Aggregation Improve Scientific Peer Review Quality?
Short Hypothesis: Single-pass LLM review is a poor match for human committees. We systematically compare **pipelines**: independent multiple samples, self-consistency, debate between two agents with a judge, and round-robin critique–response—using only automatic metrics and OpenReview alignment where available. The contribution is a **computational study of collective intelligence for review**, not a single prompt tweak.
Related Work: Multi-agent debate literature targets general QA or math, not long-document peer review. Review aggregation in ML venues is human-only. PanelBench transfers multi-agent methodology to review with domain-appropriate prompts and evaluation via ReviewForge/SkepticBench metrics—filling a clear gap.
Abstract: We introduce PanelBench, evaluating whether multi-agent workflows improve AI peer review on fixed paper sets. Pipelines include: (i) k independent reviews + median score, (ii) debate with assigned roles (skeptic vs. advocate) + synthesis, (iii) iterative revision of a draft review after simulated rebuttal. We measure faithfulness, OpenReview score alignment when labels exist, SkepticBench detection rates, and consistency across runs. We analyze cost–quality tradeoffs (API calls per paper). Results clarify whether deliberation reduces variance, catches more injected issues, or merely averages errors—guiding practical system design for conference-scale tooling.
Experiments: 1) **Datasets**: Overlap subset of papers from ReviewForge + SkepticBench injections on a shared list. 2) **Pipelines**: Implement 4–5 protocols with fixed token budgets where possible. 3) **Metrics**: Same suite as above; statistical comparison across pipelines. 4) **Ablations**: With vs. without explicit role prompts; with vs. without access to figure for VLM track.
Risk Factors and Limitations: API cost scales with agents—mitigate with smaller open models for ablations. Optimal hyperparameters may vary by model; we report best-effort grid over temperature and agent count.

Name: revtrace_temporal
Title: RevTrace: Stability and Drift of LLM Peer Review Across arXiv Paper Revisions
Short Hypothesis: Real submissions evolve; a trustworthy AI reviewer should update judgments coherently when authors fix issues. Using **public v1/v2/v3 arXiv version chains**, we quantify **score drift**, flip rate (accept→reject), and whether SkepticBench-injected flaws are resolved across versions—without any human annotation. This yields a novel **temporal reliability** axis missing from static benchmarks.
Related Work: Static benchmarks dominate; a few works study model stability on paraphrases but not real author revisions. RevTrace leverages natural revision structure, connecting to robustness and alignment literatures with a clear story for ML conferences.
Abstract: We construct RevTrace from arXiv papers with multiple versions and stable identifiers. For each version chain, we generate AI reviews and extract scores and structured judgments. We measure: (1) monotonicity—whether scores track intended improvements on injected defects when we simulate fixes by reverting injections; (2) natural drift—variance of scores across real versions; (3) inconsistency—contradictory statements about the same claim across versions. We compare models and correlate drift with magnitude of text diff. RevTrace characterizes whether models behave like calibrated reviewers over time or amplify noise.
Experiments: 1) **Data**: Mine arXiv version histories (e.g., CS categories); filter chains with ≥2 versions and sufficient diff size. 2) **Simulated fixes**: On injected SkepticBench variants, apply automatic 'fix' edits to create paired before/after. 3) **Metrics**: Score delta, semantic consistency of review text across versions (embedding similarity), detection of resolved vs. persisting issues. 4) **Baselines**: Strong single-shot vs. PanelBench-lite (two-pass) to see if deliberation helps temporal coherence.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Not all version changes are quality improvements; we separate simulated injection fixes (ground truth) from observational analysis of real chains. arXiv API rate limits require careful batching.

Name: metareview_reflexivity
Title: Reflexive Review: Quantifying Topic-Driven Bias When AI Reviews Research on AI Peer Review
Short Hypothesis: Models may exhibit **reflexive bias**: systematically different tone or scores when the manuscript topic touches LLMs, benchmarks, or peer-review automation—creating a conflict of interest in meta-level research. We operationalize this with a **matched-paper design**: same venue/style/topic area, differing only in whether the abstract mentions AI-for-review or not (careful templating or contrastive pairs from arXiv). Evaluation is fully automatic (score deltas, sentiment, claim polarity).
Related Work: Sycophancy and position bias are studied in chat; meta-level bias in scientific review is underexplored. We connect to normative concerns at AI conferences where such papers are common—offering empirical grounding rather than speculation.
Abstract: We study whether LLM reviewers treat papers about AI-assisted peer review differently from closely matched control papers. We build pairs of manuscripts (or minimally edited variants) that differ primarily in framing of AI tools in the abstract/introduction while holding methodology sections similar where possible. We measure distributional shifts in assigned scores, recommendation labels, and lexical emphasis (e.g., over-praise of benchmark contributions). We test multiple models and prompt styles. Reflexive Review documents a subtle but important deployment risk for automated meta-research evaluation and suggests disclosure or blinded prompts as mitigations—without requiring human subjects.
Experiments: 1) **Pair construction**: Template-based edits on existing arXiv papers or synthetic sections combined with real bodies—ensuring ethical use of public text only. 2) **Models**: Same slate as ReviewForge. 3) **Metrics**: Paired difference in scores; statistical tests across pairs; optional stance classifier on review text. 4) **Controls**: Paraphrase-only edits without topic shift to isolate framing effect.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Perfect matching is impossible; we report sensitivity analyses. Synthetic framing may feel artificial—we combine with a subset of naturally occurring topic pairs from keyword filtering.
